---
title: "What Tighter Corn Acres Mean for Cattle Feeders in 2026"
date: "2026-02-18"
description: "Projected shifts in corn planted area will ripple into feedlot break-evens and placement decisions this spring."
categories: [Feed Markets, Feedlot Economics, Corn]
image: "thumbnail.png"
---

*Replace with your own analysis. The structure below shows how to embed R output.*

The USDA's Prospective Plantings report signals a [X]-million-acre shift in corn acreage this year. For cattle feeders, the downstream effects on corn prices, feedlot cost of gain, and break-even calculations are worth thinking through now — before spring placements.

## The Supply Picture

[Your analysis here. You can pull USDA NASS or CME data directly in R:]

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Example: corn price trend from FRED or USDA
# library(tidyverse)
# library(fredr)
#
# fredr_set_key("your_key")
# corn <- fredr(series_id = "CORN") |>
#   filter(date >= "2020-01-01") |>
#   ggplot(aes(date, value)) + geom_line(color = "#5a7a5c") + ...

cat("Embed your R analysis here.")
```

## Feedlot Break-Even Implications

At current feeder cattle prices and a corn price assumption of \$[X]/bu., cost of gain works out to roughly \$[X]/cwt. for a 150-day closeout. [Continue your analysis...]

## What Cow-Calf Producers Should Watch

Feeder cattle demand at the sale barn is sensitive to feedlot margins. If corn stays elevated through summer, expect [your conclusion]...

---

*Questions or comments? [Email me](mailto:andrewemeryanderson@gmail.com).*
